name: Second Chechen War
summary: The Second Chechen War was fought from 1999 to 2009 between the Russian Federation and pro-Moscow Chechen forces on one side and Chechen separatist/Islamist insurgents on the other. It began after incursions into Dagestan and renewed Russian operations in Chechnya, and formally ended when Russia lifted the Chechen counter-terrorist operation regime on April 16, 2009. Casualty figures are highly contested; the best-supported range is roughly 35,000 to 77,000 total deaths, with most civilian deaths attributable to Russian/federal and allied Chechen government operations.

sides.name: Russian Federation and pro-Moscow Chechen forces
members: Russian Ministry of Internal Affairs troops

military_killed.notes: Official Russian army figures were lower and incomplete; independent estimates including police, Interior Ministry, FSB and allied Chechen units were higher. The point estimate follows the Hoover estimate of about 8,000 Russian military and police killed.

breakdown.victims_group: Chechen civilians and other civilians in Chechnya
breakdown.notes: Amnesty reported estimates of up to 25,000 killed since 1999, many in early aerial bombardment and fighting; Kramer estimated 40,000-45,000 civilians killed by the mid-2000s. Attribution is not perfectly separable, but human rights reporting identifies federal/pro-Moscow operations as the dominant cause.
Reporting citing Memorial/HRW gave as many as 10,500 civilian deaths during the winter 1999-2000 storming of Grozny. This is treated as part of the wider direct civilian-death total, not an addition.

breakdown.victims_group: Civilians in Staropromyslovski district, Alkhan-Yurt, Novye Aldi/Chernorechie
breakdown.notes: HRW documented at least 38 civilians killed in Staropromyslovski, at least 17 in Alkhan-Yurt, and at least 60 in Novye Aldi/Chernorechie.
HRW reported that Russian military aircraft strafed a civilian convoy on October 29, 1999, killing between 50 and 100 people.
HRW reported over 100 civilians killed in the October 21, 2000 attack on Grozny's central market.

civilians_killed_directly.notes: Direct civilian deaths are dominated by Russian/federal and pro-Moscow Chechen bombardment, artillery, sweep operations, executions, and indiscriminate or disproportionate force. Figures overlap across sources and are not cleanly additive.

deaths_from_actions.victims_group: Civilians and suspected insurgent supporters disappeared after abduction, arrest, detention, or sweep operations
deaths_from_actions.notes: Memorial, cited by Amnesty, estimated 3,000-5,000 people disappeared in Chechnya since 1999; Amnesty stated that state agents were allegedly responsible in the majority of cases. Some of these deaths likely overlap with broader civilian-death totals.

deaths_from_actions.victims_group: Detainees in filtration camps and ad hoc detention sites
deaths_from_actions.notes: Amnesty reported systematic torture and ill-treatment in filtration camps and cited official Russian figures that 15,000 people had been detained by late summer 2000, but a separate death toll cannot be reliably isolated.
Large-scale displacement and infrastructure destruction are documented, but no defensible separate indirect mortality estimate was found.

sides.name: Chechen separatist and Islamist insurgent forces
members: foreign jihadist fighters including Khattab-linked networks

military_killed.notes: The low reflects rebel-acknowledged losses by 1999-2004; the point follows the Hoover estimate of 8,000 Chechen separatist fighters killed; the high reflects Russian-reported militant deaths for the intense early period.

breakdown.victims_group: Russian civilians in Buynaksk, Moscow, and Volgodonsk
breakdown.notes: RAND attributes the September 1999 apartment bombings to Chechen jihadists and gives 293 killed. Attribution is disputed, and Chechen leaders denied involvement.
Chechen militants seized the theater; most hostage deaths occurred after Russian forces used gas, so attribution is causally mixed.

breakdown.victims_group: Beslan school hostages, mostly children and parents
breakdown.notes: Militants linked to the Chechen separatist insurgency seized the school; later reporting gives 334 civilian deaths including 186 children.

breakdown.victims_group: Passengers and crews of two Russian domestic flights
breakdown.notes: Female suicide bombers linked to Chechen insurgent networks destroyed two airliners in August 2004.

breakdown.victims_group: Civilians and mixed civilian/security targets in Russia and Chechnya
breakdown.notes: RAND states that Chechen suicide attacks during 2000-2004 killed over 200 people, and lists individual attacks including 41 killed in a Moscow subway bombing and 44 killed in a Stavropol commuter train bombing.

civilians_killed_directly.notes: This range includes major civilian and mixed-target insurgent attacks associated with the Chechen conflict in Chechnya, the North Caucasus, and Russia proper. Some events have contested attribution or mixed causation.

totals.notes: Totals are approximate and not mechanically precise because civilian direct-death estimates overlap with disappearances, and some insurgent attacks outside Chechnya have mixed causation. Rounded headline range: about 35,000-77,000 total deaths.

key_atrocities.name: Storming and bombardment of Grozny
key_atrocities.perpetrator: Russian Federation and pro-Moscow Chechen forces
key_atrocities.notes: Reporting citing Memorial/HRW gave as many as 10,500 civilian deaths during the winter 1999-2000 assault on Grozny; HRW described the city center as systematically destroyed.

key_atrocities.name: Staropromyslovski district killings
key_atrocities.victims_group: Civilians in Staropromyslovski district of Grozny
key_atrocities.notes: HRW documented at least 38 civilians summarily executed between late December 1999 and mid-January 2000.

key_atrocities.name: Alkhan-Yurt killings
key_atrocities.notes: HRW reported at least 17 civilians murdered after Russian forces took control of the village.

key_atrocities.name: Novye Aldi/Chernorechie massacre
key_atrocities.victims_group: Civilians in Novye Aldi and Chernorechie
key_atrocities.notes: HRW reported at least 60 civilians summarily executed on February 5, 2000.

key_atrocities.name: Fleeing convoy attack on road to Ingushetia
key_atrocities.notes: Russian military aircraft strafed a civilian convoy on October 29, 1999.

key_atrocities.name: Grozny central market attack
key_atrocities.notes: HRW reported over 100 civilians killed in the October 21, 2000 attack.

key_atrocities.name: Russian apartment bombings
key_atrocities.perpetrator: Chechen separatist / Islamist insurgent forces, disputed attribution
key_atrocities.notes: RAND attributes the bombings to Chechen jihadists; attribution remains disputed.

key_atrocities.name: Moscow Dubrovka theater hostage crisis
key_atrocities.notes: Hostage deaths followed the seizure of the theater by Chechen militants and the Russian rescue operation using gas; causation is mixed.

key_atrocities.name: Beslan school siege
key_atrocities.victims_group: Schoolchildren, parents, teachers, and other hostages
key_atrocities.notes: More than 330 hostages died; later reporting gives 334 civilian deaths including 186 children.

uncertainty_notes: Casualty data are fragmentary and politicized. Russian official military counts undercount by excluding some services and allied Chechen units; insurgent deaths rely partly on Russian claims and rebel acknowledgements. Civilian deaths, disappearances, and detention-related deaths overlap. Several major insurgent attacks outside Chechnya are linked to the conflict but have contested attribution or mixed causation, especially the 1999 apartment bombings, Dubrovka, and Beslan.
